Root Cause (first observed failure):

action.hypothesis: `residual_topk` did not fail on quality or runtime grounds; it failed because the execution layer still does not recognize the architecture name. The smallest informative next move is a narrow code-fix round that registers and wires `residual_topk` into the existing config/class dispatch and forward path so the prototype can finally receive one real K=128 proxy measurement under the current strongest regime.
action.summary: Register and implement the `residual_topk` architecture in `sparsify/`, then rerun the same K=128 proxy prototype under the current best signum settings.
action.self_review: This is the minimal blocker-removal step after a concrete architecture-registration crash, and it keeps the round focused on making the intended new family runnable rather than changing the experimental target. The main risk is that a second incompatibility appears after registration, but that is still better handled as a code-fix path than by abandoning the family without a valid signal.
notes_to_memory: Round 57 produced a concrete execution-layer blocker for `residual_topk`: the architecture name is still missing from `SparseCoderConfig` validation and/or SAE class dispatch, so the family has no usable quality signal yet.
This is stronger evidence of incomplete code integration than of a bad architecture, so `residual_topk` should not be judged on quality yet.
The next round keeps the original `residual_topk` K=128 proxy target fixed and changes only the code needed to make the family selectable and runnable by the existing launcher.
next_hypotheses: If `residual_topk` runs and shows promising proxy FVU, keep the family alive and stabilize its internal K split next.
If it runs cleanly but is clearly worse than gated and not better than factorized-topk, archive it quickly and move to another architecture family.
If `residual_topk` still fails after registration is fixed, treat the remaining issue as a deeper implementation incompatibility and shift effort elsewhere.
